text:
You are building an admin product catalog page for an e-commerce team.

The catalog is read-heavy and should feel fast for day-to-day browsing, so avoid re-querying product data on every request.

Admins can trigger a "Sync latest catalog" action from the page when upstream ERP/PIM data changes (pricing, inventory, availability). After submitting, they should be able to continue working immediately, even if the product list is briefly stale.

The expected behavior is that product data is refreshed in the background and becomes up to date shortly after, across any views that depend on the same catalog data.

Implement the sync trigger as a regular HTML form that uses an inline Server Action in the page.
Use the cache tag name "products" consistently for catalog caching and invalidation.

In practice, sync jobs can touch thousands of SKUs, so operators prioritize a responsive admin experience and eventual consistency across catalog views over forcing every request to block on freshly recomputed data.

I’m going to inspect the catalog/admin code to find the page, its data loading path, and the current caching/revalidation setup, then I’ll wire the sync form into that flow without making requests block on refresh.

I’ve confirmed this is a small Next app, so I’m narrowing in on the page and any existing server/data layer before changing behavior. Next step is to read the app files and see where to add the form and tag-based invalidation.

I found a minimal app with just a placeholder home page and a mock product source. I’m checking the current Next.js caching API now so I can use the right tag invalidation mode for stale-while-revalidate instead of accidentally forcing a blocking refresh.

I have the implementation path: cache the product loader under the `products` tag, and make the page submit a Server Action from a regular form that calls `revalidateTag('products', 'max')` so catalog views stay responsive and refresh in the background. I’m editing those two files now.

The page is now wired to the shared `products` cache and the sync button is a standard form posting to a Server Action. I’m running a quick build check next to catch any Next.js/server-action typing issues, especially around the cache invalidation call signature.
Build is still running; I’m waiting for the result so I can fix anything surfaced by Next rather than assuming the action/cache wiring compiled cleanly.

text: Next modified `tsconfig.json` during the build by adding a dev types include. That’s incidental to the feature, so I’m removing that noise and leaving only the catalog changes.
I’ve restored the unrelated config drift. I’m doing one final status check so I can report exactly what changed and whether anything remains uncommitted beyond the intended catalog work.

Implemented the catalog sync flow with tag-based background invalidation.

What changed:
- [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx)
  - Replaced the placeholder home page with an admin product catalog UI.
  - Added a regular `<form>` that submits to an inline Server Action.
  - The Server Action calls `revalidateTag("products", "max")`, which marks the catalog cache stale without forcing the current request to block on recomputation.
- [lib/db.js](/home/user/workspace/lib/db.js)
  - Wrapped product loading in `unstable_cache(...)` with the shared tag `products`.
  - Expanded the mock product records to include inventory and availability so the page has realistic catalog data.

Verification:
- `npm run build` passed successfully.

Resulting behavior:
- Read-heavy catalog views reuse cached product data.
- Clicking “Sync latest catalog” triggers background refresh via the `products` tag.
- Operators return to work immediately while dependent views converge to fresh data shortly after.
